pages.full_text:
In the morning we managed to harness Tinder into) Eve's Brompton saddle handlebar bag & go on his first bike ride. We ran about 6 of the 16 miles. Simon had him on a lead when he ran. Gorgeous scenery. Not many people. We looked around Dolgellau & had a good lasagna lunch. Blues & greys & green & brown & yellow. It was hard work cycling up the Estuary against the wind, but we were blown home. It was the Mawddach Bike Trail - a disused railway. The last bit up to the campsite was an exhausting 20 minute climb with the bikes. Tinder yapped at sheep while crossing the field.
    Poorer weather later in the day. We walked up to look at 2 yurt + a catamaran in the woods. A group from Mochant[leth]
pages.notes: Text begins mid-entry (continues from previous spread). Two inks: black at top ('In the morning…lunch.'), then blue/teal for the rest. 'Tinder' appears to be a dog's name. 'Mochantleth' is likely Machynlleth — the word is cut off at the page edge; could also read 'Mochantleth' or 'Mochantlelh'. The parenthesis after 'into)' appears to be a slip of the pen.

pages.full_text:
[plum jam label from Dolgellau]

were doing a survey - how to make the camp fire shore communal. I suggested a long fire, for people to look out across the Estuary.
    We moved over to a better pitch (£2 extra) overlooking the sea more. Glorious view. We had hoped for another walk in the morning, but it was windy & rainy[so]
photo_captions: MADE IN WALES / £3.15 / Cynhyrchwyd yng Nghymru
pages.notes: Page has a glued-in circular jam label printed 'MADE IN WALES / £3.15 / Cynhyrchwyd yng Nghymru'; handwritten annotation in blue ink beside it reads 'plum jam label from Dolgellau'. The top-right corner page number is partially visible — looks like it could be '8' but may be cut off (e.g. 83, 87, or 88). Text 'were doing a survey...' continues directly from the left page which ends '...A group from Mochant[leth]'. Final word(s) at bottom are cut off — reads 'windy & rainy[so]' or 'windy & rainy[/too wet]'. 'Estuary' likely refers to the Mawddach Estuary.
